items.title: Iran-US at the tipping point — 93 days, Hormuz still closed, isolated strikes resuming
items.idea: The Bond Beat flags that as of June 1, 93 days have passed since US-Iran strikes began and 54 days since the 'ceasefire' that is visibly fraying. DB's Early Morning Reid notes the market has 'never felt closer to a deal but potentially never felt closer to it all falling apart.' Isolated strikes resumed over the weekend. If the Strait of Hormuz remains closed into mid-summer, it risks 'a non-linear tipping point of economic stress.' Trump is reportedly quiet, suggesting negotiations are sensitive.
items.angle: This is the single hardest-to-price tail risk in markets right now. Every week of closure is supply erosion; but the volatility is in deal/no-deal binary. Watch Brent for the regime signal.
items.tweetA: The ceasefire that never was: 93 days since US-Iran strikes began. Isolated attacks resuming over the weekend. Trump went quiet — which either means a deal is close or negotiations just blew up. Either way, the Strait of Hormuz is still closed. $BRN $USO
items.tweetB: 'Never felt closer to a deal but never felt closer to it all falling apart.' That's the actual state of play on Iran right now. If Hormuz stays closed into mid-summer, DB says 'non-linear economic stress.' The market is not pricing that tail.

items.title: US strikes Iranian radar & telecom sites; Iran missiles hit Kuwait; Iran war adding $450 to US energy bills
items.idea: Per Mando Minutes June 1 roundup: US struck Iranian radar and telecom sites over the weekend. Iran retaliated with missile and drone strikes on Kuwait. The Iran war is now estimated to be adding $450 to US energy bills. Japan's crude reserves saw their steepest plunge ever. Meanwhile Trump said he is 'not in a hurry for an Iran deal.' Separately, France seized a sanctioned Russian tanker.
items.angle: Two separate belligerent acts (US strike + Iran counterattack on Kuwait, a US ally) in the same 48-hour window. Markets open to $90+ WTI. Energy infrastructure escalation risk is building.
items.tweetA: US hits Iranian radar sites. Iran shoots missiles at Kuwait. Japan crude reserves plunge most ever. That's three simultaneous energy supply shock signals in one morning. And WTI is at $90. $XLE $USO $VLO
items.tweetB: The Iran war is adding $450 to US energy bills and counting. Trump says he's 'not in a hurry' for a deal. At some point this stops being a negotiating tactic and starts being a supply crisis. June is when we find out.

items.title: Chicago PMI explodes from 49.2 to 62.7 — 2nd largest surge on record since 1967
items.idea: The May Chicago PMI jumped 13.5 points to 62.7, the second-largest single-month surge in the index's history going back to 1967. Only July 2020's pandemic rebound (+14.5 pts) was bigger. The current level is the highest in more than four years and stands well above the 54.3 long-run average. DiMartino Booth describes it as explosive but possibly short-lived. Preview of today's ISM Manufacturing print.
items.angle: Chicago PMI is often the most volatile regional survey and can diverge sharply from national ISM. But a +13.5 pt move historically signals something real is shifting in manufacturing. Watch ISM at 10am for confirmation.
items.tweetA: Chicago PMI: 49.2 to 62.7 in one month. +13.5 points. Second largest single-month surge in 60 years of history. Only pandemic rebound 2020 was bigger. ISM Manufacturing hits at 10am. If it confirms — the manufacturing recession narrative is dead.
items.tweetB: The market is focused on AI and Iran. Buried in Friday's data: the Chicago PMI had its second-biggest monthly surge since 1967. Highest reading in 4 years. Either a massive false signal or manufacturing just ripped back to life. $ISM $PMI

items.title: S&P +16% in two months — happened just 4 times since 1950; nine-week streak last seen late 2023
items.idea: David Rosenberg notes the S&P 500 is up nine weeks in a row (last seen late 2023) with 11 record closing highs in May alone. The April-May +16% surge has occurred just four other times since 1950. Despite US-Iran geopolitical confusion and IDF deepening Lebanon incursions, the 'roaring AI trade' swamped all risk signals. The key divergence: investor confidence is surging while CEO confidence is actually sagging — a classic late-cycle pattern.
items.angle: Four precedents in 75 years for a two-month +16% surge. The investor/CEO confidence divergence is the tell: retail and institutional investors are all-in, but the people running businesses are turning cautious. Who's right?
items.tweetA: +16% in two months. The S&P has done this just 4 other times since 1950. Each time, something had changed either structurally or speculative. We are about to find out which one this is.
items.tweetB: Investor confidence: near record highs. CEO confidence: sagging. The last time retail crowded in while executives turned cautious was... well, pick your bubble. $SPY $QQQ

items.title: Brent -19.3% in May — biggest monthly decline since March 2020; bond vigilantes circling
items.idea: Deutsche Bank's Early Morning Reid (cited in Bond Beat) notes Brent saw -19.3% in May, biggest monthly decline since March 2020 pandemic — driven entirely by hopes of a US-Iran deal. This dramatically eased stagflation fears and helped the S&P hit fresh records. But the Bond Beat's 'BondBot' points out the contradiction: equities drunk on AI Kool-Aid while the bond market stares at $94 Brent (now rebounding with Iran flare-ups), stubborn inflation expectations, and a 10yr yield back at 4.45%.
items.angle: The -19% oil move in May is priced on hope of a deal that may not materialize. Any deal failure = energy price reversal + stagflation fears return. June is the reversal test.
items.tweetA: Brent -19% in May. Biggest monthly drop since the pandemic. All of it 'deal optimism' on Iran. Now: US strikes Iranian radar sites. Iran hits Kuwait. 'Deal optimism' is doing a lot of heavy lifting right now. $BRN $CL1
items.tweetB: 'Stocks are partying upstairs while bonds are calling the fire department.' The Bond Beat's BondBot on today's market. 10yr at 4.45%. Oil rebounding. AI narrative eating everything. Until it doesn't.

items.title: JPOW warns Trump's 'stress test' will wreck public trust in Fed; 2yr yield hits largest weekly drop of 2026
items.idea: Fed Chair Powell explicitly warned that Trump's political pressure represents a 'stress test' that will damage public trust in central bank independence. Separately, the Bond Beat notes the 2yr yield posted its largest weekly decline of 2026 — momentum 'rolling over' — implying rate cuts are being priced back in. Contradicting signals: 10yr daily overbought but monthly oversold.
items.angle: JPOW publicly naming the 'stress test' is unusually direct for a Fed chair. Combine with 2yr yield dropping = the market is pricing CB capitulation even as Fed officially maintains hawkishness.
items.tweetA: Powell publicly called Trump's political pressure a 'stress test' that will 'wreck public trust in central bank.' Fed chairs don't use that language unless they're genuinely worried about their independence. Something is moving behind the scenes.
items.tweetB: 2yr yield: largest weekly drop of 2026. The bond market is pricing cuts back in — even as JPOW warns about CB independence. Either the market is right that Powell will fold, or someone's about to be very wrong.

items.title: Global Bubble Index hits new highs; GFC-style delinquency beneath; Irving Fisher 'permanently high plateau' parallel
items.idea: MacroEdge's Don Johnson argues the current asset melt-up is driven by loose financial conditions, AI earnings tailwinds, and government interventions keeping WTI near $90. Under the surface: GFC-style delinquency in consumer credit, private credit, and student loans is building. The Global Bubble Index is hitting new highs. Korean, Canadian, Spanish, Chinese equity markets are all in 'vertical' moves. MacroEdge calls this 'the all-in bubble in everything' and says 'we're quite close to the permanently high plateau period that Irving Fisher once outlined.'
items.angle: The Irving Fisher parallel is the most dangerous sentence in any macro newsletter. Fisher called the 1929 market a 'permanently high plateau' days before it crashed. MacroEdge is not predicting imminent crash — but the setup rhymes.
items.tweetA: 'We're quite close to the permanently high plateau period that Irving Fisher once outlined.' — MacroEdge. Irving Fisher said that in October 1929. Not saying the same thing is coming. But that's the sentence that should make you put your phone down and think.
items.tweetB: Global Bubble Index at new highs. Korean, Canadian, Spanish, Chinese equities all in vertical moves. GFC-style delinquency building beneath. And margin debt is still rising. The problem with melt-ups: they end suddenly. $SPY $IWM

charts.desc: MacroEdge margin debt chart + Global Bubble Index — Charts inside — worth opening

items.title: US workers' share of output lowest since 1947; corporate profits highest since 1950
items.idea: Mando Minutes June 1 cites two staggering macro data points: US workers' share of output is at its lowest level since 1947 — nearly 80 years. Simultaneously, corporate profits are at their highest share since 1950. This is the starkest bifurcation between capital and labor in modern US history, occurring in parallel with GFC-level consumer delinquencies.
items.angle: This is the structural backdrop behind 'Main Street vs. Wall Street.' If workers' share keeps falling while profits rise, the political economy consequences are enormous — tax policy, labor regulation, social stability.
items.tweetA: US workers' share of output: lowest since 1947. Corporate profits: highest since 1950. That's 75 years of redistribution from labor to capital happening simultaneously with GFC-level consumer delinquencies. The market calls this 'healthy margins.'
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to this data point: corporate profits at 75-year high, worker share at 75-year low. At some point this isn't a macro signal — it's a political signal. Watch what happens to tax policy in the next cycle.

items.title: Delinquency: 90+ day credit card 7.1% (15yr high); auto 2.97% (near 15yr high)
items.idea: Boockvar cites the NY Fed Q1 2026 data: the 90+ day delinquency rate for all consumer debt is at 7.1%, steady over the past two years but sitting at a 15-year high. Auto loan 90-day delinquency is at 2.97%, just below a 15-year high. A separate WSJ chart shows the proportion of delinquent credit card debt that is 90+ days old at 13%.
items.angle: Steady at 15-year highs is not the same as rising — but it means there's no healing. The auto delinquency at near 15yr high matters for auto sector credit, ABS, and consumer discretionary.
items.tweetA: 90-day credit card delinquency: 7.1% — 15-year high. Auto delinquency: 2.97% — near 15-year high. These have been 'steady' for 2 years. 'Steady at the highest level in 15 years' is not neutral — it means the bottom half of the consumer never healed. $AXP $COF $F $GM
items.tweetB: Corporate profits at 75yr high. Stock market at ATH. And simultaneously: auto delinquency near 15yr high, credit card 90-day delinquency at 15yr high. We are running two different economies. The one with stocks is doing great.

charts.desc: 90-day delinquency charts for credit cards AND auto loans from NY Fed/WSJ — Charts inside

items.title: Michael Burry: SpaceX + Anthropic + OpenAI IPOs = inflation-adjusted equivalent of ALL 300 dot-com IPOs in 2000
items.idea: Boockvar surfaces a tweet from Michael Burry: adjusted for inflation, the upcoming SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI IPOs will raise as much or more than all 300 internet and TMT IPOs combined in 2000. The implication is not subtle — Burry is flagging late-cycle capital market behavior. This is the most direct comparison to dot-com bubble issuance from arguably the most famous bear of the last generation.
items.angle: Burry's framing is crucial: it's not that these companies are bad — it's that the scale of issuance at once is what defined peak 2000. ChartStorm also noted buybacks are fading just as big IPOs loom. The supply/demand of equity is shifting.
items.tweetA: Michael Burry: SpaceX + Anthropic + OpenAI IPOs will raise, inflation-adjusted, as much as ALL 300 dot-com IPOs combined in 2000. This is not Burry being bearish on the companies. It's Burry being bearish on the timing.
items.tweetB: Burry's IPO comparison is the number I can't stop thinking about. Three companies. Same capital raise as 300 companies at peak dot-com. Buybacks fading at the same moment. Who's buying the supply? $SPX $QQQ

items.title: Semis overbought; buybacks fading; median stock short interest at decade+ highs
items.idea: ChartStorm's weekly roundup flags: (1) Semiconductors are 'fully overbought' — haven't seen this type of scrambling into semis for a long time. (2) Buybacks have gone from a major tailwind to 'nearly irrelevant' as big IPOs loom. (3) Median stock short interest is at decade+ highs — the author isn't sure if this is shorting AI losers (software), borrowing boring stocks to fund AI longs, or directional bets. If the rally broadens, short squeeze could add fuel.
items.angle: The buyback→IPO dynamic is a structural shift in equity supply/demand. The short interest at 10yr highs is an asymmetric catalyst — either stays at record as market stalls, or explodes higher in short squeeze.
items.tweetA: Three things coexisting right now: Semis fully overbought. Buybacks nearly irrelevant. Median stock short interest at decade highs. The fuel for a melt-up AND a melt-down are both in place simultaneously. $SOX $SMH
items.tweetB: Buybacks: major tailwind → nearly zero. Big tech IPOs: imminent. Short interest: decade highs. The equity supply/demand math is about to flip. Nobody's talking about this while AI earnings print records. $SPY

charts.desc: Multiple charts — semis overbought vs history, buyback trend, short interest decade chart. Charts inside — worth opening

items.title: Survey: 2nd highest reading ever of investors expecting higher stock prices; average equity allocation at record high
items.idea: ChartStorm reports that the percentage of people expecting higher stock prices a year from now reached its second-highest reading ever in May. Simultaneously, the average investor holds a record high allocation to equities. Wall Street AI long-term earnings growth estimates are closing in on all-time highs. Goldman Sachs composite of market indicators shows levels that 'indicate we are living in unusual times.' Callum Thomas flags all of this as a growing vulnerability, while noting 'the trend is still your friend.'
items.angle: Second highest reading ever for bullish expectations. The question isn't whether to be in the market — it's what your exit signal is. Sentiment doesn't kill bull markets — but it tells you how stretched the elastic is.
items.tweetA: Second highest reading ever of investors expecting stocks to go higher in a year. Record equity allocation. AI earnings estimates near ATH. Goldman market composite 'at stretched levels.' Everyone in the pool. $SPY $VTI
items.tweetB: When the survey shows second-highest-ever bullish expectations, that's not a 'the market will keep going up' signal. That's a 'who's left to buy?' signal. Sentiment doesn't kill bull markets — but it tells you how stretched the elastic is.

charts.desc: 4 charts on sentiment/positioning extremes. Charts inside — worth opening

items.title: SPX closes ATH every day last week — but more stocks declined than advanced each day (longest such streak since 2024)
items.idea: Hi Mount Research flags a striking breadth divergence: the S&P 500 closed at new all-time highs every single day last week, yet every day had more stocks declining than advancing within the index. This matches the longest such daily breadth losing streak since 2024. The broader cross-asset risk appetite indicator shows an 'ongoing surge in risk-on behavior' — but the rally is increasingly narrow.
items.angle: Index at ATH every day + breadth negative every day is the classic warning sign that the rally is concentrated in a few large caps. In extremis this leads to a sudden stop when those names rotate.
items.tweetA: S&P 500: new all-time high every day last week. Also S&P 500: more stocks declined than advanced every single day last week. Both things are true. That's the narrowest ATH streak since 2024. $SPY vs $RSP
items.tweetB: Breadth vs index: the market's biggest lie detector. When the index makes new highs but most stocks fall, you're watching 5-10 names carry 500. The question is always: when does the weight get redistributed. $NVDA $META $AAPL

items.title: Implied correlation at 6.33 (3rd percentile of 2yr range, -69% YoY) — it's a stock picker's market
items.idea: The Gamma Report notes COR1M is at 6.33 — the 3rd percentile of its two-year range, down 69% YoY. Below 8, stocks trade on fundamentals. Above 8, macro drives everything. 'IBM is up 17% because of IBM. BSX is down 15% because of BSX.' The signal to watch is when correlation starts moving back toward 8 with high rate of change — that means macro is about to override fundamentals. NFP Friday is the most likely catalyst.
items.angle: Low correlation = stock-picking alpha is real right now. The moment correlation spikes back toward 8, individual stock selection becomes irrelevant and you need portfolio hedges.
items.tweetA: Implied correlation at 6.33 — 3rd percentile of the 2-year range. This is the options market saying 'IBM and BSX have nothing to do with each other right now.' It's a stock picker's market. Concentration and hedging don't work the same way. $COR1M
items.tweetB: Every spike in implied correlation this year has lined up with a market drawdown. We're at 3rd percentile (6.33). The risk isn't a broad selloff — the risk is being in the wrong stock. NFP Friday is the next potential macro override. $SPX

items.title: NFP May est. 85K vs prior 115K; AVGO Wednesday — most important AI capex signal of the week
items.idea: This week is anchored by: NFP Friday (85K est vs 115K prior — confirmation of labor deceleration; beat above 115K resets the narrative; wages at 0.3% MoM + soft payrolls = 'stagflation-lite story the market will not like') and Broadcom (AVGO) Wednesday after close ('most important earnings print of the week' — AVGO's AI revenue guidance is 'one of the cleanest forward indicators of hyperscaler infrastructure spend').
items.angle: Two binary events in one week. AVGO determines the AI buildout continuation trade. NFP determines whether the Fed-cut narrative lives or dies heading into June/July.
items.tweetA: Two events this week that tell you everything: AVGO earnings (Wed) — cleanest read on whether hyperscaler AI capex is accelerating or plateauing. NFP Friday at 85K est — if it misses badly, the stagflation-lite read comes back. Both matter. $AVGO $SPX
items.tweetB: Nonfarm payrolls estimate: 85K. Prior: 115K. A beat above 115K resets the rate cut narrative. A wages print of 0.3% alongside weak jobs = stagflation signal. Friday could be the first serious reality check for this market in weeks.

items.title: CFTC approves first US bitcoin perpetual (Kalshi BTCPERP) + Coinbase offshore perps path
items.idea: On May 29, the CFTC approved Kalshi's BTCPERP — the first regulated US bitcoin perpetual contract, cash-settled, 24/7, with a funding mechanism tied to spot. Simultaneously, it issued a no-action letter allowing Coinbase Financial Markets to route eligible US customers to options and perps at the global level via its Bermuda subsidiary (Deribit connection). Coinbase +7%, Robinhood +11% on Friday.
items.angle: US perps are now legal. This is the gap Hyperliquid dominated. Markets immediately voted 'validates' for HYPE — the CFTC legalizing perps is bullish for the category leader.
items.tweetA: The CFTC just did something crypto has waited years for: approved the first regulated US bitcoin perpetual. Kalshi's BTCPERP. Cash settled. 24/7. Funding rate. This is the product that's been running billions of daily volume offshore for years. Now it's legal here. $COIN $HOOD $HYPE
items.tweetB: Most important crypto regulatory event since the Bitcoin ETF: CFTC opens US perp markets. Two paths: domestic (Kalshi), offshore (Coinbase via Bermuda/Deribit). Robinhood +11%, Coinbase +7%. The market is pricing a structural expansion, not substitution.

items.title: HYPE hits ATH $73.50, +30% from Thursday lows; outperformed Bitcoin by 270% YTD
items.idea: Lucky Trader notes Hyperliquid's HYPE token jumped 30%+ from Thursday lows to a new all-time high at $73.50 after the CFTC news. HYPE has now outperformed Bitcoin by 270% on the year, cementing itself as 'the primary crypto market darling of the cycle.' The market decisively answered whether CFTC perps are good or bad for Hyperliquid — legal perps in the US is bullish for HYPE.
items.angle: HYPE at ATH while BTC ETFs bleed $1.4B in a week is the most interesting capital rotation signal in crypto right now. Money leaving BTC ETFs, going into DeFi perpetuals infrastructure.
items.tweetA: HYPE hits $73.50 ATH. +270% vs Bitcoin YTD. The narrative: US legalizes perps, which means more competition for Hyperliquid. The market's actual answer: Hyperliquid IS the perps market. Competition validates, not dilutes, the category leader.
items.tweetB: BTC ETFs: $1.4B outflows in a week, worst streak in history. HYPE: new ATH. KNTQ: +55% on the week. The rotation in crypto is not from crypto to stocks. It's from BTC exposure to AI/perps infrastructure bets. $BTC $HYPE

items.title: Bitcoin ETF $1.42B outflows — 3rd worst week ever; 10-day consecutive outflow streak (longest on record)
items.idea: Spot BTC ETFs recorded $1.42B in net outflows last week (May 25-29), the third-highest weekly outflow since launch in January 2024. BlackRock's IBIT alone saw $966M in weekly outflows. The outflow streak hit 10 consecutive days — the longest on record. Total BTC ETF net assets fell to $94.17B. Capital rotating to alts: HYPE ETFs drew $25.57M, XRP ETFs +$15M, Solana ETFs +$3.36M.
items.angle: This is NOT a crypto-exits-market signal. It's an intra-crypto rotation signal. Institutional ETF holders rotating from BTC to alt exposure — a maturity signal.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin ETF flows: $1.42B outflows last week — 3rd worst week ever. 10-day consecutive outflow streak — longest ever. BlackRock IBIT alone: -$966M. But HYPE ETFs are at +$25.57M. This is not 'crypto is dead.' This is rotation. $IBIT $FBTC
items.tweetB: Buried in the Bitcoin ETF outflow panic: Solana ETFs getting inflows. XRP ETFs getting inflows. HYPE ETFs getting inflows. The ETF infrastructure we built for BTC is now being used to express diversified crypto allocation. That's actually bullish.

items.title: Dimon vs Armstrong: Clarity Act stablecoin yield fight; 56% odds of passing; Senate floor vote in 30 days
items.idea: JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon called Brian Armstrong 'full of shit' over the Clarity Act's stablecoin yield provision. Armstrong wants the 'bona fide activities' exception allowing yield on stablecoin balances. Dimon says any yield = deposit competition threatening banks' funding base. Bill cleared committee 15-8 with bipartisan support May 14. Needs 60 Senate votes (~6 Dem crossovers). Floor vote in 30 days. Prediction markets: 56% odds of passing.
items.angle: Dimon vs Armstrong is a proxy battle for whether stablecoins compete with bank deposits. If stablecoins can pay yield, why hold a savings account? This is the banking lobby's existential fight.
items.tweetA: Jamie Dimon calling Brian Armstrong 'full of shit' in public. The underlying fight: can stablecoin platforms pay yield? Banks say no — it threatens their deposit base. 56% odds it passes. Senate floor in 30 days. $COIN $JPM
items.tweetB: The real stablecoin battle isn't crypto vs SEC. It's crypto vs banking deposit franchise. If USDC can pay 5% yield, why does Chase exist for savings? Dimon knows this. That's why he's fighting so hard. Watch Clarity Act in June.

items.title: US seizes ~$1B Iranian crypto; FBI seizes $8B+ (largest crypto seizure ever)
items.idea: Treasury Secretary Bessent confirmed the US has 'outright grabbed' roughly $1B worth of crypto from Iran. Separately, the FBI has seized $8B+ in crypto — the largest single crypto seizure in history. Also: a Texan ran a $12.3M AI-bot crypto fraud (SEC case). The government is demonstrating functional capability to identify, track, and seize crypto at scale.
items.angle: State-level crypto warfare is now real and operational. If Iran can have $1B seized, the 'crypto is unseizable' narrative is officially dead.
items.tweetA: The US just 'outright grabbed' $1B in Iranian crypto. The FBI seized $8B+ — largest crypto seizure ever. The 'government can't touch your crypto' narrative was always wrong. It just took them a while to build the infrastructure. That infrastructure is now built.
items.tweetB: $8B seized by FBI. $1B grabbed from Iran. A Texan ran a $12.3M AI-bot crypto fraud. The crypto crime and sanctions enforcement story is scaling as fast as the legitimate market. KYC/AML infrastructure is about to get a lot of government attention.

items.title: Solana tokenomics civil war: SIMD-547 (fee burn 16-100x) vs SIMD-0411 (cut 22.5M SOL / ~$4.5B over 6 years)
items.idea: SIMD-547 (resource-based fee burn): daily burns rising from 648 SOL to 10,800-64,800 SOL (16x-100x increase). SIMD-0411 (Helius researchers): doubles disinflation rate from -15% to -30%, cutting 22.5 million SOL (roughly $4.5B) from emissions over six years and reaching terminal 1.5% inflation much faster. Anatoly Yakovenko is actively engaging with both designs.
items.angle: SIMD-547 = fee burn model (Ethereum EIP-1559 style). SIMD-0411 = supply reduction model (Bitcoin-style). They aren't mutually exclusive. Anatoly engaging with both means both could pass.
items.tweetA: Solana is having its monetary policy moment. SIMD-547: burn 16-100x more fees per day. SIMD-0411: cut 22.5M SOL emissions (roughly $4.5B) over 6 years. Anatoly engaging with both. Ethereum did this with EIP-1559. Look what happened to ETH after. $SOL
items.tweetB: 648 SOL burned per day → up to 64,800 SOL per day. That's the potential impact of SIMD-547 on Solana. Combined with SIMD-0411's supply cut, this is the most aggressive tokenomics shift SOL has ever contemplated.

items.title: Circle freezes $12.6M cUSDC via court order — blacklist risk in 'confidential' DeFi exposed
items.idea: A US district judge ordered Circle to blacklist Zama's confidential USDC contract (cUSDC), freezing $12.6M in pooled user funds. Because cUSDC wraps USDC in a single pool, blacklisting the contract froze every user's funds — not just the disputed party. Zama CEO: protocol was 'caught in crossfire' — over 99% of contract funds came from the disputed deposit.
items.angle: This breaks the 'confidential DeFi' thesis: if your privacy wrapper holds USDC and the US judiciary can order Circle to blacklist the wrapper, your privacy has a legal override switch.
items.tweetA: A US court just froze $12.6M in Zama's 'confidential' USDC contract with a single order to Circle. The privacy layer is built on top of a permissioned token. When the court reaches the USDC layer — the privacy layer is irrelevant. Legal risk in 'confidential DeFi' is real.
items.tweetB: Circle blacklisted an entire pool of 'confidential' USDC because one disputed transaction used it. 99% of the pool was innocent bystanders. This is the systemic risk of privacy primitives built on permissioned stablecoins.

items.title: European power grid double shock: Qatar LNG offline (Hormuz) + historic drought → Dunkelflaute crisis risk this winter
items.idea: Doomberg lays out a 'perfect storm' for the European power grid: (1) Qatari LNG facilities 'hopelessly offline on the wrong side of the Strait of Hormuz,' removing key dispatchable gas supply; (2) water levels across Europe have 'fallen to lows not seen in years,' threatening hydro output. When intermittency exceeds dispatchability, grid operators have nothing left to load-match. Europe's winter Dunkelflaute (days with simultaneously low wind and solar) could become a crisis point.
items.angle: The California analogy is instructive: gas declining + solar rising works until it doesn't. Europe added intermittency assuming Nordic hydro as backstop. Both are now simultaneously compromised.
items.tweetA: Doomberg maps the European grid setup heading into winter: Qatar LNG offline (Hormuz still closed) + worst drought in years cutting hydro. Europe built its grid assuming Nordic hydro = battery backup. That battery is running dry. $NG $TTF
items.tweetB: You want the most under-discussed energy risk of 2026? It's not oil. It's European natural gas power this winter. LNG supply cut off. Hydro reservoirs draining. Wind/solar intermittency with no dispatchable backup. Dunkelflaute season starts October.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (California gas vs solar, European grid data)

items.title: Iran has maximum leverage on global energy markets; pain threshold is WTI above $100
items.idea: MacroEdge argues Iran is sitting with 'significant leverage': the longer they hold out and countries draw down emergency reserves, the worse prices get long-term. WTI at ~$92 is artificially suppressed by government intervention. MacroEdge: 'The setup is really constructive if acute shortages begin hitting global economies from June onward.' Pain range for markets is a return above $100/bbl WTI.
items.angle: Iran understands the game theory: every week of impasse depletes strategic reserves. If the deal collapses in June, the market is in a worse supply position than in March. This is why $100 WTI is a regime-change level for equities.
items.tweetA: Iran's strategy is simple: run the clock. Every week the Hormuz closure continues, strategic reserves drain. By June, the world is in a worse supply position than April. $100 WTI is the level that changes everything for equities. MacroEdge says Iran knows this.
items.tweetB: WTI at $92 because governments are jawboning prices down and releasing reserves. That's not a market price — that's a political price. When reserves run out, you see the real price. The question is timing, not direction. $USO $XLE

items.title: Jensen Huang declares '2026 the year of agents' from Taiwan; AI not reducing jobs — creating more software engineers
items.idea: In a speech from Taiwan, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang called 2026 'the year of agents' — framing the current AI cycle as the agent deployment wave, not just training/inference. He explicitly addressed the jobs debate: 'People talk about AI reducing jobs — complete nonsense. It's causing more software engineers to be hired.' S&P 500 futures opened the month higher on this backdrop.
items.angle: The 'year of agents' framing is Huang setting the narrative for the next 12 months of AI capex. Agents run 24/7, use tools, scale horizontally — a different compute demand curve than query-response.
items.tweetA: Jensen Huang, Taiwan speech June 1: '2026 is the year of agents.' Not models. Agents. The distinction matters for the capex cycle: agents run 24/7, agents use tools, agents scale horizontally. That's a different compute demand curve than query-response. $NVDA
items.tweetB: 'AI is not reducing jobs — it's causing more software engineers to be hired.' — Jensen Huang. This is either the most important data point for the labor market narrative or the most motivated reasoning from a man selling chips. Possibly both.

items.title: Nvidia RTX Spark Arm-based PC chip debuts (MSFT/Dell/HP); memory stocks surge (MU, SDISK)
items.idea: Two AI hardware signals: (1) Nvidia debuting RTX Spark — an Arm-based PC chip for laptops from Microsoft, Dell, and HP — bringing NVDA into the consumer PC market where AMD and Intel dominate. (2) Memory stocks rising pre-market, with Micron (MU) and Samsung SDISK leading. The memory move may be driven by AI inference demand or anticipated Nvidia PC chip memory spec.
items.angle: NVDA entering PC/laptop market is a new front in the silicon wars. If RTX Spark has AI acceleration baked in, this competes with AMD MI laptop chips and Intel Lunar Lake. Microsoft+Dell+HP launch partners = serious channel.
items.tweetA: Nvidia just opened a new front: Arm-based PC chips for laptops. Microsoft, Dell, HP as launch partners. RTX Spark. This is NVDA vs AMD (Ryzen AI) vs Intel (Lunar Lake) in the consumer PC market. Memory stocks rising pre-market on the news. $NVDA $AMD $INTC $MU
items.tweetB: Nvidia in the PC chip market was a when, not an if. Now it's when: today. RTX Spark. The AI PC market was AMD vs Intel. Now it's three-way. And NVDA brought its own GPU architecture to the fight.

items.title: CFM's Bouchaud: 'From one day to one year, flows drive markets — not fundamentals'; CFM grows to $27B AUM
items.idea: Rupak Ghose's deep dive on CFM (Paris, $27B AUM) surfaces Jean-Philippe Bouchaud's core thesis: in the short run — one day to one year — fund flows trump fundamentals entirely. He told Barry Ritholtz: 'The whole bull run is because of an influx of money.' This formalizes the Gabaix-Koijen 'Inelastic Markets Hypothesis' (Harvard/Chicago, 2020). CFM added nearly $10B AUM in one year — one of fastest-growing globally. Growing via iCapital and Stifel retail partnerships.
items.angle: If Bouchaud is right, the AI bull market is primarily a flows story — not an earnings story. The corollary: when flows reverse, fundamentals won't save you.
items.tweetA: CFM's Bouchaud has spent decades studying this: 'In the short run — one day to one year — it's really flows that matter.' Not earnings. Not fundamentals. Flows. The Inelastic Markets Hypothesis. And right now, flows are all going into equities. $SPY
items.tweetB: 'The whole bull run is because of an influx of money.' — Jean-Philippe Bouchaud, chairman of $27B hedge fund CFM. This is not bearish — it's analytical. Bull markets run on flows until flows reverse. The question: what reverses them.

items.title: AI agents fail on architecture not intelligence; open-weight models now at frontier level at fraction of cost
items.idea: 0xJeff shares 60 days of building AI agents. Key finding: agents fail on architecture — tools fighting each other, not model intelligence. Ran through 5-6 provider setups in 60 days. Conclusion: 'Open-weight labs are measuring up to frontier labs in intelligence while keeping the cost low.' Best practice: pick one provider and stick. Direct API saves 5-10 seconds vs multi-hop routing. Exa > manual search; Browser CDP > Playwright (Cloudflare blocks headless).
items.angle: The commoditization of intelligence is confirmed by practitioners, not just benchmarks. If you can't tell the difference between DeepSeek v4 and Claude in production workflows, the moat narrative for frontier models needs re-examination.
items.tweetA: 60 days of building AI agents. The failure mode isn't model intelligence — it's architecture: tools fighting each other, slow routing, bad memory retrieval. Open-weight models are now at frontier performance. The moat is workflow design, not raw capability.
items.tweetB: DeepSeek API gave 75% discount before making it permanent. Multi-hop routing adds 5-10 seconds of latency. Open-weight models = frontier quality, 1/10th cost. The enterprise AI model pricing war is already being fought at the API layer.

items.title: ECB's Schnabel: digital euro needed; China weighs digital yuan clearing house; Vietnam eyes crypto for SME loans
items.idea: Three CBDC/stablecoin developments: (1) ECB's Schnabel publicly stated the digital euro is 'needed' — possibly as counter to USD stablecoin dominance (Fed's Waller had said stablecoins 'extend US reach'). (2) China weighing a digital yuan clearing house. (3) Vietnam eyeing crypto as collateral for SME loans. Together these show the global CBDC/stablecoin race accelerating.
items.angle: The Fed's Waller 'stablecoins extend US reach' thesis vs ECB's 'digital euro needed' is the monetary geopolitics of the decade. If USD stablecoins dominate global commerce, the euro loses monetary influence.
items.tweetA: Fed's Waller: stablecoins 'extend US reach.' ECB's Schnabel: digital euro 'needed.' China: weighing digital yuan clearing house. Vietnam: crypto as SME loan collateral. The global monetary sovereignty battle is being fought at the stablecoin layer. $USDC $USDT
items.tweetB: Schnabel calling for a digital euro is the ECB saying 'USD stablecoin dominance is a threat.' The stablecoin is the new dollar reserve currency battle. Watch carefully.

items.title: DTCC selects Stellar for tokenization; Coinbase launches INR rails in India; Binance plans US stock trading for non-US users
items.idea: Three infrastructure expansion signals: (1) DTCC (US clearing backbone) selected Stellar for tokenization — extremely significant as DTCC clears virtually all US securities. (2) Coinbase launched direct INR rails in India. (3) Binance announced plans for non-US users to trade US stocks on its platform.
items.angle: DTCC + Stellar is the sleeper item. This is not DeFi. This is the US financial plumbing experimenting with blockchain settlement. If it goes live at scale, the implications for settlement costs, timing, and access are enormous.
items.tweetA: DTCC — the company that clears virtually every US security — just selected Stellar for tokenization. Not DeFi. Not crypto. The infrastructure backbone of US capital markets running on a public blockchain. If this scales, it rewires settlement. $XLM
items.tweetB: Coinbase launches INR rails in India. Binance plans US stock trading for global users. DTCC picks Stellar. All in one day. The financial plumbing integration is accelerating. These aren't crypto stories — they're financial infrastructure stories.

items.title: Software (IGV) +6.25% Friday; small caps up 70% from 2025 low; Bitcoin 'less convincing'
items.idea: ChartStorm notes software stocks (IGV) surged +6.25% on Friday. Small caps (IWM) are up 70% from their 2025 low and just hit weekly overbought territory — in 2020-21 the similar run went to +150% before ending. Bitcoin, by contrast, is 'looking less convincing' vs software stocks. IWM 70% from low in 2020-21 = midpoint of the eventual 150% run.
items.angle: Software bouncing is the 'AI losers' trade reversing. If AI makes productivity tools less valuable, why are enterprise software companies at new highs? Either the thesis was wrong, or this is the last squeeze.
items.tweetA: IGV (software ETF) +6.25% Friday. The sector everyone declared dead from AI. Small caps up 70% from the 2025 low. Equal-weighted S&P broke out. The breadth story just got interesting. This is either the bull broadening or the last squeeze before the turn. $IGV $IWM
items.tweetB: Bitcoin unconvincing. Software surging. Unusual pair. And IWM: up 70% from 2025 low — in 2020-21, the same point was the middle of a run that went to +150%. Either there's more, or this time is different.

charts.desc: Software vs Bitcoin chart — worth opening

items.title: Canada macro 'worsening, not improving' — real estate to commodities pivot thesis under pressure
items.idea: Danny Dayan updates his Canada macro thesis: September 2025 ('The North's Potential') — promising; November 2025 ('Frosty North') — murkier; June 2026 ('Northern Downgrade') — 'the picture seems to be worsening, not improving.' The original thesis: Canada could reorient from real estate to commodities to capitalize on the resource boom. That transition is not playing out as hoped.
items.angle: Canada as a commodity/resource play was a popular macro theme. If it's breaking down, that matters for Canadian equities, CAD, and the broader energy/materials positioning thesis.
items.tweetA: Canada macro: the 'pivot from real estate to commodities' thesis has gone from promising (Sept 2025) to murky (Nov 2025) to 'worsening, not improving' (June 2026). The resource reorientation story for Canada is not working as scripted. $EWC $CAD
items.tweetB: Watching the Canada macro thesis deteriorate over 9 months: Danny Dayan's June update is 'Northern Downgrade.' Worsening. This matters for CAD, Canadian banks, and anyone long the commodity Canada thesis.

items.title: CFM: 75% employee-owned, Blue Owl 25%; $27B AUM; academic model (RenTec-like); retail expansion via iCapital/Stifel
items.idea: CFM (Capital Fund Management, Paris) profile: employees own 75%, Blue Owl Capital holds 25%. Near one-third of headcount has PhDs in physics/math. Academic partnerships with Imperial, ENS, École polytechnique, Columbia. CFM grew ~$10B in one year to $27B AUM — matching QRT's 2-year growth rate. New channel: retail access via iCapital and Stifel partnerships. Three of five board members have PhDs in physics.
items.angle: CFM growing at QRT pace is remarkable. The iCapital retail push follows the quant hedge fund playbook of the 2020s — democratization of alternatives. More European quant firms doing US retail distribution is the trend.
items.tweetA: CFM went from ~$17B to $27B AUM in roughly one year. Paris-based, professor-run, 75% employee-owned. Matching QRT's growth rate. Now expanding to retail via iCapital + Stifel. The French quant firms are quietly eating the allocator world. $BX
items.tweetB: 'We don't hire from business schools. We hire people who have done good science.' That's Simons at RenTec. It's also CFM's culture. The best-performing hedge fund structures share one thing: treat investing as physics, not finance.

items.title: DocSend 2024: 2.5% conversion from investor meeting to close; VCs evaluate founder-market fit above traction
items.idea: DocSend 2024 fundraising data: average seed founder contacts 58 investors → 40 first meetings → 15 second conversations → 3-4 term sheets → 1 close (2.5% conversion rate). First Round Capital's 2024 founder survey reveals top factors VCs assess in first meetings: (1) founder-market fit, (2) quality of thinking under pressure. Both rank above traction, metrics, and pitch deck quality.
items.angle: The 2.5% conversion rate means 97.5% of first meetings end without a term sheet. The meeting confirms the prior view — it doesn't form it. Preparation = research on the investor, not rehearsal of the pitch.
items.tweetA: Seed fundraise math: 58 investors → 40 meetings → 15 second convos → 3 term sheets → 1 close. 2.5% conversion. What VCs evaluate: founder-market fit and thinking under pressure — ABOVE traction. The deck is the least important thing in the room.
items.tweetB: By the time you walk into a VC meeting, they've already formed a view. The meeting confirms or contradicts it. 97.5% of first meetings produce no term sheet. Preparation = research on the investor, not rehearsal of the pitch. The best founders know the difference.

items.title: The $500K to $5M quant gap: stat-arb books return 2-4% GMV regardless of PM — capital allocation is the variable
items.idea: Young & Calculated argues the gap between a $500K and a $5M quant year is NOT intelligence. A statistical arbitrage book returns roughly 2-4% on GMV regardless of who runs it. The variable is capital behind the seat (pod math, 10-30% payout range) and drawdown rules at Millennium, Citadel, and Point72. The meta-skill is winning the internal capital allocation game, not generating alpha.
items.angle: The insight generalizes: in any levered business (hedge fund pod, trading desk, PE firm), return on intellectual capital is bounded by financial capital allocated to it. Getting capital is the meta-skill.
items.tweetA: A quant stat-arb book returns 2-4% on GMV regardless of who runs it. The $500K vs $5M difference isn't who's smarter — it's who gets $1B behind their book vs $100M. Getting capital allocated is the actual alpha. The intellectual alpha is table stakes.
items.tweetB: Millennium. Citadel. Point72. The drawdown rules at each decide how long you keep a book. It's not about Sharpe ratio — it's about surviving long enough to compound. Capital allocation within a pod shop is the ultimate performance measurement.
